title: Red Flags When Hiring a Web Designer
excerpt: The web design industry is full of talented professionals — but also plenty of operators who overpromise, underdeliver, and disappear after payment. These are the red flags you should never ignore when hiring a web designer.
content:
Most web design projects go smoothly when you hire the right person. But some end in frustration, financial loss, and a half-built website you can't use. Here are the warning signs to watch for before you commit.

🚩 They Can't Show You Live Work

A designer who only has mockups, screenshots, or designs "in the works" — but no live, functional websites you can visit — is a red flag. Live work is the only proof that a designer can take a project from concept to launch.

🚩 Pricing Is Vague or Comes With No Written Scope

A reputable designer provides a detailed proposal outlining exactly what is and isn't included. Vague pricing like "starting from $X" without scope definition is an invitation for disputes and scope creep.

🚩 They Promise Unrealistic Results

"We'll get you to page 1 of Google in 30 days." "Your sales will double in a month." SEO and business growth take time. Anyone promising guaranteed, fast results is either naive or dishonest.

🚩 They Don't Ask About Your Business

A designer who jumps straight to design without understanding your goals, target audience, competitors, and brand values is designing in a vacuum. Great web design is strategic — it starts with questions, not software.

🚩 Communication Is Already Slow

If they take 3 days to respond to a pre-sales enquiry, imagine how responsive they'll be when you're in the middle of a project. Communication speed during the sales process is a reliable preview of the working relationship.

🚩 No Contract or Formal Agreement

Never start a project without a signed contract. A written agreement protects both parties, defines ownership, and sets expectations. Any designer unwilling to sign a contract is a serious risk.

🚩 Ownership of Your Website Is Unclear

Make sure your contract explicitly states that you own all design files, code, and the domain. Some unscrupulous operators retain ownership to keep clients dependent on them.

🚩 No Post-Launch Support Plan

What happens if your website breaks after launch? A professional designer or agency has a defined support process. If they can't answer this question, you'll be on your own when problems arise.
